summary: Patrick McKenzie argues urgently that businesses running Rails 2.3 applications face certain security compromise since Rails Core has dropped support, and presents Rails LTS — a commercially supported fork by German consultancy Makandra — as the practical solution. He explains that upgrading to Rails 3/4 is prohibitively expensive for many businesses, self-maintaining a fork requires expertise most companies lack, and relying on informal forks like GitHub's offers no guarantees. McKenzie bankrolled Rails LTS as its anchor customer with $10,000, negotiating a model where patches are open-sourced after a 10-day delay. The post systematically dismantles objections to paying for the service, framing it as insurance rather than paying for code.
key_points: Rails 2.3 is completely unsupported and every unpatched Rails 2.3 server will inevitably be compromised — this is not a risk but a certainty
Upgrading from Rails 2.3 to Rails 3 is a serious engineering project that can cost $20,000 to six figures, making it impractical for many businesses with legacy apps
Rails Core declined to support 2.3 even when offered $10,000, which is their prerogative as an OSS project funded by companies with different priorities
Rails LTS provides a commercially supported fork with contractual guarantees on patch timelines, costing ~$200/month — roughly 1-2 engineer-hours
The arrangement benefits the community: all patches are open-sourced under MIT license after a 10-day delay, creating something that didn't exist before
money_quotes: If any server operated by your company is on an unpatched version of 2.3, that server will be compromised by the adversary.

You're not really paying money for OSS code which already exists, you're buying the guarantee that new code gets written at non-deterministic times in the future before the lack of it causes an emergency for your business.
Data security is serious business for many people.
It also could very well result in firings, so I wouldn't rush to this option.

The hypothetical person saying this is a textbook pathological customer: they're both deeply irrational and likely to be far, far, far too much headache for professional Rails engineers to have to deal with.
But horsepuckey, you're not going to write free patches for Rails 2.3, or you'd have commits you could point to demonstrating your willingness to do this over the last 6 months.

You can either pay your engineers to continue monitoring and patching Rails for you, or you can buy Rails LTS. Your call. Their entry-level plan costs ~$200 a month, which is approximately the cost of one or two engineer-hours.
Or you can just wait until your server gets owned, because that reliably makes people discover budget for security.
key_insight: When open source projects drop support for versions your business depends on, paying for commercial maintenance is not a luxury but a basic cost of doing business — far cheaper than the inevitable security breach.
